patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to cover various race conditions between qgroup deletion and squota writes. The tests aim to identify potential bugs in the handling of extent ownership references (EXTENT_OWNER_REF) when a subvolume is deleted or its qgroup is destroyed. The scenarios tested include freeing an extent whose owner qgroup is gone, creating an extent while the owning subvolume is being deleted, destroying a live subvolume's qgroup with delayed refs, and destroying a qgroup belonging to a still-mounted subvolume. The patch ensures that these operations are properly handled by the Btrfs kernel to prevent underflows or transaction aborts.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention and release events. The patch uses an arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, only a single NOP instruction is added to the hot path, minimizing performance impact. When enabled, the contention check, trace call, and unlock are combined in an out-of-line function to further minimize hot path impact. The patch also includes estimates of binary size increases due to this change.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already unlocked, and suggested adding a check to ensure the lock is locked before tracing.

The reviewer suggested removing a phrase from the patch description that is considered unnecessary in Git change logs.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and may cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of returning immediately, the function should be called directly

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out a redundant call to queued_spin_release() in the code and requested its removal.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:34 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Remove the ", introduced in the previous commit," That's useless in git
change logs.

---

Get rid of the "return;". What does it save you? It just makes it that you
need to duplicate the code. Even though it's a one liner, it can cause bugs
in the future if this changes. You could call the function:

  do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock);

---

And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra pointed out that the patch adds unnecessary instructions to the hot path, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the initial NOP, increasing binary size by 12-15 bytes. He also disagreed with the claim that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n configurations opt against binary size optimization on x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Peter's concern about the size increase of the generated code due to the added tracepoint. The author provided detailed assembly code comparisons between the baseline and patched versions, showing that while the total size increase is 9 bytes, the executed path only adds a 2-byte NOP instruction, which has special treatment in CPU.

Author acknowledges that the original binary size increase was due to a single 2-byte NOP instruction and asks if this change alleviates the reviewer's concerns.

Author acknowledged a characterization error in the patch description regarding UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK on x86_64 and agreed to correct it
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Peter. This is exactly the kind of feedback I
was looking for.

I understand your concerns and initially I had exactly the same
thoughts, and after I looked into the generated code more carefully the
impact on the executed path is smaller than the total size increase
suggests.

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() for baseline (before the patch) is
31 bytes in total (x86_64, defconfig, GCC 11).

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() with tracepoint (after the patch
applied) is 40 bytes in total.

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (NOP, static branch)
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

It is 40 bytes (+9 bytes compared to baseline, 2 bytes for NOP and 7
bytes for CALL and JMP).

But if we look at the executed path the picture is a bit different.

Baseline, in best case scenario of least number of executed
instructions.

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock,
                                           ; always executed)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Tracepoint (again same case of least number of executed instructions).

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (always executed, this is an
                                           ; only addition on the execution path).
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock, always executed)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

On the execution path we are getting 21 byte worth of instructions on
baseline against 23 bytes. The only addition on any executed path is the
2-byte NOP, that has a special treatment in CPU, cheap, but not entirely
free.

---

a single 2-byte NOP.

Does this change the picture for you, or is the NOP still a concern for
this path?

---

Fair point on the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization, but
UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCKis always "y" on x86_64. The inlined case only
applies to s390 (unconditionally), csky and loongarch (when
!PREEMPTION). I'll remove this, thanks.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a feedback about adding a comment to explain why the tracepoint is enabled only when contended_release is enabled, agreeing to make the change.

The author addressed a concern about the performance impact of tracing on x86_64, explaining that combining the trace and unlock in an out-of-line function minimizes hot path impact by avoiding callee-saved register save/restore.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, will do here and in other places.

[...]

---

This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
with GCC 11).

Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
  
    endbr64
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
  
    endbr64
    push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
    mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  unlock                      ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    pop  %rbx
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already held, and requested a check to be added before calling queued_spin_release_traced()

Reviewer Steven Rostedt expressed concern over duplicated code in queued_spin_unlock() and suggested adding comments to explain its purpose, specifically mentioning the need for synchronization if changes are made to this location.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 14 May 2026 14:13:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Ah, because the return makes it into two tail calls.

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about
needing to update the other location if anything changes here? And perhaps
comment that this duplicate code helps the assembly.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a potential point of confusion in the patch and agreed to improve documentation by adding more comments to explain the shared release logic between traced and non-traced paths.
review_comments.raw_body:
My idea was that queued_spin_release() serves the same role that the old
queued_spin_unlock() had: a pure lock-release primitive without tracing.
That was the primary motivation for extracting queued_spin_release() in
the first place (it is just one line of code), so the common release
logic between the traced and non-traced paths is shared explicitly
rather than duplicated semantically.

I agree that this could be explained better. I'll add more comments
there to clarify the rationale. Thanks for the suggestion, Steve.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to include calls to trace_contended_release(). This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

This is a preparatory step for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation in the next commit.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long a lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Peter Zijlstra noted that the patch adds unnecessary instructions (12-15 bytes) to the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the original MOV instruction, which he considers 'quite ludicrous'. He also disagreed with the assumption that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size on x86, stating that the unlock is smaller than a function call.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Peter's concern about the size increase of generated code due to the patch, explaining that while the total size is larger, the executed path only adds a 2-byte NOP instruction, which has a special treatment in CPU and is not entirely free.

Author asks if a single 2-byte NOP is still a concern in this path after their previous changes

Author acknowledged a concern about the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization being specific to certain architectures and agreed to remove it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Peter. This is exactly the kind of feedback I
was looking for.

I understand your concerns and initially I had exactly the same
thoughts, and after I looked into the generated code more carefully the
impact on the executed path is smaller than the total size increase
suggests.

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() for baseline (before the patch) is
31 bytes in total (x86_64, defconfig, GCC 11).

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Generated code of _raw_spin_unlock() with tracepoint (after the patch
applied) is 40 bytes in total.

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (NOP, static branch)
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

It is 40 bytes (+9 bytes compared to baseline, 2 bytes for NOP and 7
bytes for CALL and JMP).

But if we look at the executed path the picture is a bit different.

Baseline, in best case scenario of least number of executed
instructions.

    3e0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    3e4:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock,
                                           ; always executed)
    3e7:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    3ee:  je   3f5                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    3f0:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    3f5:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    3fa:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

Tracepoint (again same case of least number of executed instructions).

    bc0:  endbr64                          ; 4 bytes (always executed)
    bc4:  xchg %ax,%ax                     ; 2 bytes (always executed, this is an
                                           ; only addition on the execution path).
    bc6:  movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; 3 bytes (unlock, always executed)
    bc9:  decl %gs:__preempt_count         ; 7 bytes (always executed)
    bd0:  je   bde                         ; 2 bytes (always executed)
    bd2:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes (executed if above
                                           ; je is not taken)
                                           ; rest is not executed
    bd7:  call queued_spin_release_traced  ; 5 bytes
    bdc:  jmp  bc9                         ; 2 bytes
    bde:  call __SCT__preempt_schedule     ; 5 bytes
    be3:  jmp  __x86_return_thunk          ; 5 bytes

On the execution path we are getting 21 byte worth of instructions on
baseline against 23 bytes. The only addition on any executed path is the
2-byte NOP, that has a special treatment in CPU, cheap, but not entirely
free.

---

a single 2-byte NOP.

Does this change the picture for you, or is the NOP still a concern for
this path?

---

Fair point on the UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK characterization, but
UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCKis always "y" on x86_64. The inlined case only
applies to s390 (unconditionally), csky and loongarch (when
!PREEMPTION). I'll remove this, thanks.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a suggestion to add a comment explaining why the contended_release tracepoint fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

The author addressed Steven Rostedt's concern about the performance impact of using a separate function for the contended_release tracepoint, explaining that three extra instructions on the hot path are wasted when the tracepoint is off and justifying combining the trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the suggestion, will do here and in other places.

[...]

---

This is the scenario the comment above the static branch describes.
Here's what it looks like in practice on x86_64 (defconfig, compiled
with GCC 11).

Current design (trace + unlock combined, with return):
  
    endbr64
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rdi)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  preempt_handling            ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

With the trace-only function (no return, unlock after the call):
  
    endbr64
    push %rbx                        ; saves callee-saved rbx (!)
    mov  %rdi,%rbx                   ; preserve lock across call (!)
    xchg %ax,%ax                     ; NOP (static branch)
    movb $0x0,(%rbx)                 ; unlock
    decl %gs:__preempt_count
    je   preempt
    pop  %rbx                        ; callee-saved restore (!)
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk
    call queued_spin_release_traced  ; cold
    jmp  unlock                      ; cold
    call __SCT__preempt_schedule
    pop  %rbx
    jmp  __x86_return_thunk

Three extra instructions marked by "!" on the hot path (push, mov, pop),
all wasted when the tracepoint is off. That's the main reason for
combining trace and unlock in the same out-of-line function.

review_comments.summary:

I still don't like the duplication, perhaps add some more comments about

review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the complexity of replacing global NODE_DATA with a new interface and suggested using alloc_context to extend existing functionality.

review_comments.author: Brendan Jackman
review_comments.summary: Clarified the intention to use alloc_context and suggested carving out a distinct node data instance for testing purposes.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri May 15, 2026 at 3:48 PM UTC, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Fri, May 15, 2026 at 09:43:02AM +0000, Brendan Jackman wrote:
>> 
>> Yeah, I have had a similar thought before. In fact, I wonder if we could
>> have a pointer in there that effectively allows you to replace
>> NODE_DATA? I think that would be a more general mechanism to achieve
>> that `managed_node` thing?
>>
>
> Well, alloc_context already contains a nodemask.  I could see even
> pulling that argument into the struct if we seriously consider exporting
> alloc_context.
>
> I'll have to think about the NODE_DATA replacement.  I don't know if
> that's really feasible consider that this structure is used statically
> all over the kernel for runtime node-data lookups from pages/folios.

Oh yeah I wasn't thinking of replacing it completely, more that the
global NODE_DATA is the "default" node data (and the pointer field being
NULL would probably mean to use that), but then private node datas could
also exist.

>> My original motive for that was: if we could get the allocator to stop
>> [unconditionally] mutating global variables it would make it easier to
>> test.
>> 
>
> Can you expand on this a bit more?
> What globals are you referred to exactly?

IIRC it's just a few, the vast majority of the global mutable state is
ultimately downstream of NODE_DATA, plus there are some tuning knobs.

> There has been a desire on our side (Meta) to make mm/ more testable in
> general (for both performance and correctness) - include page_alloc.c
>
> But with everything so tightly coupled the best we can presently do is
> runtime testing of benchmarks and workloads.
>
> The same issue exists for things like LRU/MGLRU, where you can't really
> isolate a change because you get emergent properties.

Yeah basically just this; if you try to write a test that constructs
some specific condition to try and hit the nastiest possible fallback
case, you are racing against the real system. Ditto if you wanna check
your code against page_group_by_mobility_disabled=1, well you'd better
figure out a way to actually boot a whole system with that property.

I spent a few days trying to solve this in the way that VMA and xarray
etc have, i.e. by making the page allocator a library and then testing
it from userspace. I think that would work but it needs much more than a
few days to make it happen (admittedly, I had tried to do this with AI
at the time and it failed miserably. Maybe with today's models it would
work, which could massively accelerate the grunt work of e.g. splitting
stuff into new files).

But anyway, if you could carve out a distinct node data etc you could just
write KUnit tests that operate on a completely isolated "instance" of
the allocator, even though it still runs in a real kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/3] fuse: fix race between ring creation and connection abortion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a race condition between ring creation and connection abortion in the fuse driver. The issue arises when a thread creates a ring while another thread is aborting the connection, leading to a deadlock. The fix involves checking if the connection is still active before publishing the ring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/3] fuse: fix race between registration and connection abortion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition between the registration of an io_uring command and connection abortion in the FUSE filesystem. The issue arises when a thread attempts to register a command while another thread is aborting the connection, leading to a deadlock. The patch introduces a check for the connected state under a spinlock, ensuring that the queue reference count is properly decremented and the stop waitqueue is woken up.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that it addresses the race condition and is technically sound.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/15/26 06:55, Joanne Koong wrote:
> This fixes this race:
> - thread a: io_uring_enter -> register sqe ->
>   fuse_uring_create_ring_ent -> allocate ent but doesn't grab queue_ref
>   yet
> - thread b: fuse_conn_destroy() -> fuse_chan_abort() ->
>   fuse_uring_abort() is a no-op due to queue ref being 0
> - thread a: grabs the queue_ref, queue_ref is now 1, rest of
>   fuse_uring_do_register() logic executes
> - thread b: fuse_chan_abort() returns, fuse_chan_wait_aborted() now runs
>   and calls
>   "wait_event(ring->stop_waitq, atomic_read(&ring->queue_refs) == 0);"
> The abort/unmount thread will hang indefinitely in unkillable state as
> nothing will decrement queue_refs or wake stop_waitq, and the ring,
> queue, and ent are leaked.
> 
> Fix this by checking fch->connected under fch->lock after the created
> ent has grabbed a ref count on the queue. This ensures that in the
> scenario above, it is guaranteed that we either release the queue ref
> and wake up stop_waitq (in case fuse_chan_wait_aborted() is already
> waiting) in fuse_uring_do_register() when we detect !fch->connected, or
> if the connection is aborted after the check, it is guaranteed that the
> async teardown worker will be running in the background cleaning up ents
> and decrementing the ent's ref on the queue, which will unblock the
> eventual queue and ring teardown.
> 
> Fixes: 24fe962c86f5 ("fuse: {io-uring} Handle SQEs - register commands")
> Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 3/3] fuse: fix moving cancelled entry to ent_in_userspace list
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation of io_uring, where cancelled entries were being moved to the ent_in_userspace list and causing a crash when dereferenced. The fix involves freeing the entry and dropping queue_refs directly in fuse_uring_cancel(), which is safe because cancel is the cancel handler itself. Additionally, the patch removes a gate that prevented fuse_uring_abort() from flushing requests and stopping queues if there were still queued requests, as cancelled entries may have already dropped queue_refs.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a daemon dies or an IO_URING_F_CANCEL is called, and suggested calling fuse_abort_conn() in fuse_uring_cancel() to properly abort the connection
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm, ok, I had done that via fuse_uring_entry_teardown(), but this way
is also fine.

While thinking about it over night, I wonder if we should abort the
connection here. Calls for fuse_uring_cancel() / IO_URING_F_CANCEL
happen when

a) The daemon dies - that is what I had written the function for
b) When one calls

With reduced rings queues we would actually need to have per queue refs
and if a single queue reaches 0, it would need to re-calculate the
queue. In general this gets complex and from my point of view, if
fuse-server wants to re-initialize queues, fuse-server should:

a) wake up the ring thread with an eventfd (libfuse already has that)
b) we need a reconfig SQE (like FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_RECONFIG) that
requests to re-configure things

Right now that is all not supported, from my point of view we should
call fuse_abort_conn() when we call into fuse_uring_cancel()


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern that the patch may introduce a deadlock due to the io_uring submitter's task context holding the uring lock, which could lead to issues when calling io_uring_cmd_done(). The author agrees that keeping things simple by just aborting might not be the best approach and thinks it might lead to more trouble.
review_comments.raw_body:
fuse_uring_cancel() since the cancel runs in the io_uring submitter's
task context and the uring lock is held when it gets called. The abort
logic can trigger calls to io_uring_cmd_done(cmd, -ENOTCONN,
IO_URING_F_UNLOCKED) (through fuse_uring_stop_queues() ->
fuse_uring_entry_teardown()) which will lead to a deadlock in trying
to acquire the lock that's already held. I like the idea of keeping
things simple with just aborting, but I think in actuality it might
lead to more trouble.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert noted that the patch introduces a persistent root of trouble in the form of a lock, and expressed a lingering temptation to create an async task to tear down the connection instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah agreed, this lock is a persistent root of trouble :) I'm still
tempted to create an async task to tear down the connection here.


Thanks,
Bernd

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes several issues in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation for io-uring, a Linux I/O interface. The patches address races between fuse ring creation and abort, registration and abort, and moving cancelled entries to an in-userspace list. These issues can cause indefinite hangs during unmount and resource leaks. The approach taken involves modifying the FUSE code to ensure that request flushing always happens when cancelling operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/5] iova: switch to augmented rbtree for log(n) allocation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the IOVA allocator to use an augmented rbtree that tracks free gaps between nodes and their subtrees, allowing for log(n) allocation time instead of O(n). The new data structure maintains two additional fields per node: gap_to_prev (the free gap between this node and its predecessor) and __subtree_max_gap (the maximum gap in the subtree rooted at this node). A new search function walks right-first to prefer high addresses, pruning subtrees with smaller gaps. This change aims to address performance issues under heavy fragmentation, where the original O(n) allocation time could take 20+ seconds and trigger softlockups.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/5] iova: drop dead cached_node / cached32_node infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused and redundant infrastructure for cached_node and cached32_node in the iova (I/O Virtual Address) code. The fields were maintained on every insert and delete operation but never consulted during allocation. The useful logic from __cached_rbnode_delete_update() is preserved by moving it inline into remove_iova(). This change simplifies the code and removes unnecessary complexity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/5] iova: limit_pfn-aware augmentation for log(n) 32-bit alloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of DMA allocation in Linux by introducing a new augmented field that bounds the search by 32-bit-clamped gap size. This allows for O(log n) allocation time even when dealing with mixed-DMA-mask domains, which previously degraded to O(n). The change is achieved by precomputing and storing the clamped gap size on each iova node, enabling pure function callbacks without domain context. The update also maintains both the original and new augmented fields, allowing for seamless transition between 32-bit and 64-bit allocations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/5] iova: alignment-aware two-phase search for log(n) aligned alloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of IOMMU allocation by introducing a two-phase search algorithm in the __iova_search_free_gap function. The first phase prunes the tree based on alignment-aware bounds, reducing the number of nodes to visit and making the search O(log n). If this phase fails, the second phase falls back to the existing behavior, which is O(n) but only triggers when no suitable gap exists in the tree. This approach ensures that allocations are aligned correctly while minimizing the number of nodes visited during the search process.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/5] iova: add KUnit test suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a KUnit test suite for the augmented-rbtree IOVA allocator. The test suite includes various test cases to verify the correctness of the allocator, including domain lifecycle, allocation and deallocation, alignment, and invariant checking. The tests are designed to catch any sequencing bugs in the insert, erase, rotate, and propagate operations. The patch also adds a helper function to verify the invariants of the IOVA tree.
